coredata.dc:title: Intrinsically fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for sensing Cu2+ in aqueous media and constructing an IMPLICATION logic gate
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shi Gang
Li, Na
Fan, Yu Zhu
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: We have demonstrated a fluorescence approach for rapid, environment-friendly, and selective detection of Cu2+ in aqueous media using a kind of easily prepared and autofluorescent polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) as a sensor. The PNPs were synthesized by crosslinking hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) with formaldehyde in water solution, and they emitted strong intrinsic fluorescence without the conjugation to any external fluorescent agent. The Cu2+ assay was on the basis of the principle that Cu2+ can be adsorbed on the surface of the PNPs, resulting in the fast and remarkable fluorescence quenching through electron transfer. The developed sensor was applied to the analysis of copper ions in environmental water samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the PNPs-Cu2+ system can restore upon addition of cysteine (Cys) because of its ability to remove Cu2+ from the surface of PNPs. Based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” conversion, a reversible IMPLICATION logic gate was designed by using Cu2+ and Cys as the two inputs, and fluorescence intensity of the PNPs as the output signal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer nanoparticles
Fluorescent sensor
Copper ions
Cysteine
IMPLICATION logic gate

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of polymer nanoparticles
Sensing strategy and fluorescence quenching mechanism
Optimization of experimental parameters for Cu2+ sensing
Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
Cysteine-induced fluorescence enhancement and IMPLICATION logic gate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of PNPs, Cu2+ detection and IMPLICATION logic gate operation based on the PNPs.

(A) TEM image (inset shows a single magnified nanoparticle) and (B) SEM image of PNPs, (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of PNPs (inset: photograph of PNPs solution under visible light), (D) normalized fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PNPs solution (inset: photograph of PNPs solution under UV light of 365nm), and (E) FT-IR spectra of hPEI and PNPs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PNPs solution (curve a), PNPs solution containing Cu2+ (curve b), PNPs solution containing Cu2+ and EDTA (curve c), PNPs solution containing Cu2+ and Cys (curve d), hPEI solution (curve e), and hPEI solution containing Cu2+ (curve f). Conditions: PNPs, 0.4% (v/v); Cu2+, 20μM; EDTA, 20μM; Cys, 100μM; hPEI, 100mgL−1, HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0); Inset is photographs of PNPs solutions before (left) and after (right) addition of Cu2+ under UV light of 365nm. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of hPEI, Cu2+, PNPs, hPEI in the presence of Cu2+, and PNPs in the presence of Cu2+.
Selectivity of Cu2+ using PNPs as a sensor. The concentration of all the metal ions is 10μM. Conditions: PNPs, 0.4% (v/v); HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) fluorescence intensity changes of the PNPs in the presence of various concentration of Cu2+ (inset of B: a linear relationship of F 0/F versus the concentration of Cu2+ over the range from 0 to 8μM). Conditions: PNPs, 0.4% (v/v); HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0); excitation, 352nm.
Truth table (A), working symbol (B), and reversible switching (C) of the IMPLICATION logic gate.
Detection of Cu2+ in real samples (n=6).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The tap water was collected from our lab.

head.ce:title: Intrinsically fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for sensing Cu2+ in aqueous media and constructing an IMPLICATION logic gate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have demonstrated a fluorescence approach for rapid, environment-friendly, and selective detection of Cu2+ in aqueous media using a kind of easily prepared and autofluorescent polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) as a sensor. The PNPs were synthesized by crosslinking hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) with formaldehyde in water solution, and they emitted strong intrinsic fluorescence without the conjugation to any external fluorescent agent. The Cu2+ assay was on the basis of the principle that Cu2+ can be adsorbed on the surface of the PNPs, resulting in the fast and remarkable fluorescence quenching through electron transfer. The developed sensor was applied to the analysis of copper ions in environmental water samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the PNPs-Cu2+ system can restore upon addition of cysteine (Cys) because of its ability to remove Cu2+ from the surface of PNPs. Based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” conversion, a reversible IMPLICATION logic gate was designed by using Cu2+ and Cys as the two inputs, and fluorescence intensity of the PNPs as the output signal.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent sensors possess such advantages as high sensitivity, easy operation, and capacity for rapid, real-time detection, resulting in an enormous demand in many areas such as clinical analysis, environmental monitoring, waste management, industrial and food processing, and biomedical technology [1,2]. Therefore, the development of fluorescent sensors is a field of immense interest. In particular, the study for sensing biologically important ions and molecules has captured great attention [3–6]. Copper, an essential element for organisms, plays a key role in human health [7,8]. It is necessary for bone formation, cellular respiration and connective tissue development. However, the exposure to high level of copper ions (Cu2+) may cause disturbance of the cellular homeostasis and even damage of liver or kidney [9]. Some serious neurodegenerative diseases, such as Menkes disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and Wilson disease, might also have a close relation with copper presented in excess [10]. Consequently, the maximum allowable level of copper in drinking water permitted by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is 1.3ppm (∼20μM) [11].

In view of the importance of copper for human health, the qualitative and quantitative detection of Cu2+ is very significant in environments, pharmaceutical industry, as well as biological fields. To date, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing optical sensing platforms for the detection of Cu2+. For example, colorimetric sensors were created based on gold nanoparticles [12,13], dye-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles [14,15], et al., and also fluorescent sensors were developed based on various fluorescent species such as organic fluorophores [16,17], fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) [18,19], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [20,21], noble metal nanoclusters (NCs) [22,23], and other fluorescent materials [24–27]. Unfortunately, most of these optical sensors have some inherent drawbacks such as relatively complicated and costly synthesis, or even harmful systems, which hamper their practical applications. Therefore, the development of easily accessible and environment-friendly fluorescent sensors for Cu2+ detection is still in pursuit.

Recently, fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) have attracted increasing interest in chemical and biological applications [28,29], because they are endowed with some intrinsic advantages including low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and flexibility of synthesis. Most reported fluorescent PNPs have been created by entrapment or covalent conjugation of fluorescent agents in polymeric matrixes [30,31]. But these preparation methods generally trend to be rather tedious and time-consuming. In addition, if the PNPs are prepared just by physical entrapment, there is a risk that fluorescent agent may leak out of the nanoparticles in practical applications. Thus, ideal fluorescent PNPs should be intrinsically fluorescent without the need of embedding an external fluorochrome. In this respect, conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) are autofluorescent because they contain large π-conjugated backbones and delocalized electronic structure [32]. Furthermore, when conjugated polymers serve as fluorescent sensors, the excitation energy along the whole backbone of the polymers can transfer to a lower energy electron or energy acceptor quickly, leading to the amplification of fluorescence response signal and the high sensitivity [33,34]. However, the preparation of conjugated polymer nanoparticles requires sophisticated multistep synthetic pathways in organic solvents, which is a significant disadvantage to their applications. In our latest researches [35,36], we reported the synthesis of a kind of non-conjugated PNPs with intrinsic fluorescence emission which was prepared from hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and aldehydes, and also the intrinsic fluorescence mechanism was investigated in detail.

In this work, we used the new kind of fluorescent PNPs constructed by crosslinking of commercial hPEI with formaldehyde to develop a fluorescent sensor for detecting Cu2+. On the one hand, similar to conjugated polymer nanoparticles, the PNPs in this work can emit strong intrinsic fluorescence without the conjugation to any external fluorescent agent, and the developed sensor shows high sensitivity to Cu2+. On the other hand, the preparation of the PNPs is very facile and environment-friendly (one-pot reaction in water solution), which is an obvious merit compared to those of conjugated polymer nanoparticles or most other fluorescent materials. The fluorescence of the PNPs can be selectively quenched by Cu2+ in the pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc buffer, and it is hardly influenced by other metal ions. This is because Cu2+ can interact with the functional groups on the PNPs surface, leading to an electron transfer from the PNPs to Cu2+. The Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching makes it possible to probe Cu2+ in aqueous media. Molecular logic gates have received much attention over the past decades because they own high potential in the field of molecular-scale computers and electronics [37,38]. Interestingly, we found that the fluorescence of the PNPs-Cu2+ system could be recovered upon addition of cysteine (Cys), which was attributed to the strong binding affinity of Cys to Cu2+. Based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” conversion, a reversible IMPLICATION logic gate was successfully designed. The schematic illustration of Cu2+ sensing and logic gate operation is shown in Scheme 1 .

Hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI, Mw =10 000, 99%) and formaldehyde (35wt%) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd., Shanghai, China. Sodium hydroxide, NaCl, KCl, LiNO3, AgNO3, MgCl2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Mn(Ac)2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, CdCl2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Pb(NO3)2, and CaCl2·2H2O were supplied by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Plant (Sichuan, China). Cysteine (Cys), and other 19 amino acids including arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), aspartic acid (Asp), asparagines (Asn), glutamine (Gln), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), proline (Pro), phenylalanine (Phe), serine (Ser), tyrosine (Tyr), threonine (Thr), tryptophane (Trp), and valine (Val) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiment. Buffer solutions were prepared in accordance with standard protocols, and the pH values were measured with a PHS-3C pH meter (Leici, China). The concentrations of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer and HAc-NaAc buffer solutions are 0.04M and 0.1M, respectively.

UV-vis absorption spectra were collected by using a UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded with an F-2700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 150-W xenon lamp. A 1cm path quartz cell was used for the measurement of both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed with a JEM 1200EX transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 120kV (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization was carried out on a JSM-6510 LV scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). For SEM measurement, the sample was pasted on the holder with a conductive adhesive tape and then sputtered with a layer of platinum. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were tested using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). For the preparation of FT-IR samples, the PNPs solution was lyophilized to collect dry product, and then the fine powder grounded with KBr was pressed to form a pellet. A PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd., China) was utilized to detect pH values of solutions.

The PNPs were prepared by the crosslinking of hPEI with formaldehyde according to the previously reported method with some modification [35]. Typically, 50μL of 0.1gmL−1 hPEI was first dissolved in 445μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (0.04M, pH 5.0) by stirring for about 1min, and then 5μL of formaldehyde solution (35wt%) was added. Subsequently, after vigorous stirring for 1min again, the mixture was stirred and heated at 80°C for 4h via hydrothermal treatment. Then the as-synthesized PNPs solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff of 3000Da) for 24h. The product inside the dialysis bag was collected for further study.

A typical Cu2+ detection procedure was conducted as follows: 2μL of PNPs was added to a 1.5mL PP tube with 398μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), and the solution was mixed. Subsequently, 100μL of Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations were added to the mixtures in the PP tubes. The mixed solutions were shaken well. At last, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected under excitation at 352nm. For detection of Cu2+ in environmental water samples, the tap water was used without any pretreatment, and the river water was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 30min and then filtered through a 0.22μm membrane. Then, the water samples were spiked with various concentrations of standard Cu2+ solutions. Other procedures were the same as described above. All measurements were carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of polymer nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the PNPs is based on the crosslinking of hPEI with formaldehyde [35]. In the crosslinking process, the amphiphilic PNPs, containing amine groups on the surface and imine bonds (CN) situated in the inner, were formed. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the TEM image reveals that the PNPs are better monodisperse and exhibit near-spherical morphology with a diameter of about 150nm. A similar morphology was observed in the SEM image (Fig. 1B). The as-prepared PNPs solution is deep yellow under visible light (inset of Fig. 1C) and the UV–vis absorption spectrum presents that the PNPs have an absorption peak at 328nm (Fig. 1C), while hPEI and formaldehyde have no obvious absorption above 250nm (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, Fig. 1D shows that the PNPs can emit strong blue fluorescence with an emission peak at 465nm under maximum excitation of 352nm. The new absorption band and fluorescence emission can be ascribed to the n→π* and n⟵π* transition of CN bonds [39], respectively. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) data are exhibited in Fig. 1E. The native hPEI has absorption peaks at 2950, 2840, and 1472cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of CH2 bonds, and characteristic absorptions at 3420 and 1573cm−1 belong to NH bond. And a notable new peak located at 1645cm−1 emerges in PNPs compared with native hPEI, which is attributed to CN stretching vibration [40]. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield was found to be as high as 46%. In addition, the fluorescence could keep stable at least three months when the PNPs were stored at 4°C, suggesting that it owns a satisfied long-term storage stability. The preparation method for the PNPs was facile and environment-friendly because the PNPs were constructed by one-pot reaction without the need of organic solvents. Besides, it should be noted that the preparation was operated under a relatively low-temperature condition (80°C), which is economic and makes it more competitive compared to carbon dots that are metal-free but need a harsh synthesis condition (usually above 180°C) [18,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that hPEI, a water-soluble cationic polyamine, contains such ample primary, secondary, and tertiary amine groups that it can interact with many metal ions and shows excellent chelating ability. As a result, hPEI has been widely utilized in water treatment as a heavy metal ions adsorbent. Several previous reports have studied systemically the chelating properties of hPEI to some metal ions and the results reveal that hPEI exhibits stronger chelating ability to Cu2+ than other metal ions, especially in a lower pH (4.0) solution [42,43]. Additionally, Cu2+, as a transition metal ion with paramagnetic property and unfilled d shell, can easily quench the fluorescence of some fluorescent materials via electron or energy transfer mechanism [8,20,25]. As a result, we speculated rationally that our prepared PNPs can serve as a selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor for probing Cu2+ in aqueous media.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, just as expected, the fluorescence intensity of PNPs can be dramatically quenched by adding 20μM copper ions. In contrast, hPEI solution has no significant fluorescence before and after the addition of copper ions (curves e and f of Fig. 2A). Fig. 2B displays that the complexes of hPEI and Cu2+ have an absorption peak at 272nm and a wide weak absorption band in 500–800nm, which is in agreement with the previous report [18]. With the addition of Cu2+ to the PNPs solution, a new strong absorption peak located near 280nm and another new weak broad absorption band in the 500–800nm range were found, indicating that cupric amine coordinates were formed on the PNPs surface. However, the new absorption bands are not overlapped remarkably with the emission spectrum of the PNPs, so it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching is likely caused by electron transfer rather than resonance energy transfer. The fluorescence quenching process might be like this: firstly the amine groups on the PNPs surface chelated Cu2+, forming the cupric amine complexes; then the electrons transferred from the fluorescent groups located in the core of PNPs to the copper ions. To confirm the electron transfer process, a fluorescence quenching experiment with the addition of excess free hPEI to the PNPs solution before adding Cu2+ was carried out. As shown in Fig. S2, free hPEI reduces the fluorescence quenching, suggesting that some copper ions interact with amine motifs in the free hPEI. Thus, the copper ions are not in a close proximity with the PNPs, causing the ineffectiveness of electron transfer. To further prove the importance of the formation of cupric amine complexes on the PNPs surface to the electron transfer process, a strong metal ion chelator, ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), was added to the mixed solution of PNPs and Cu2+ to observe the fluorescence change. Fig. 2A (curve c) displays that the quenched fluorescence of the PNPs by Cu2+ can be basically recovered, which reveals that the electron transfer is eliminated by EDTA capturing Cu2+ from the surface of PNPs via a competitive complexation reaction. In addition, cysteine, a thiol-containing amino acid, also can remove Cu2+ from the surface of PNPs due to the strong binding force of Cu2+-S [41], accompanied by the fluorescence recovery (Fig. 2A, curve d). The results confirm that the electron transfer from fluorescent groups of the PNPs to Cu2+ results in the fluorescence quenching. The Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching enabled us to develop a fluorescence sensor for the measurement of Cu2+ in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental parameters for Cu2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that pH plays a key role in complexation reactions. hPEI can show the strong chelating ability to copper ions even at a low pH (4.0), while the chelating ability to other ions is relatively weaker under this pH condition. This property of hPEI implies that a good selectivity to Cu2+ would be obtained using the prepared PNPs as a sensor. Under the condition of pH 4.0, the specificity of PNPs to Cu2+ was evaluated. The fluorescence changes of PNPs were investigated in the presence of Cu2+ and other 18 kinds of cations including K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Al3+. As exhibited in Fig. 3 , most metal ions have an insignificant effect on the fluorescence intensity, except that Hg2+ may slightly quench the fluorescence of the PNPs in the pH 4.0 aqueous solution. Meanwhile, we also measured the fluorescence in the presence of common anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, Ac−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2−, and CO3 2−) and Fig. S3 displays that the 11 common anions barely influence on the fluorescence intensity of PNPs. The weak acidic condition (pH 4.0) is very important for the good selectivity. Fig. S4 shows the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0/F) under different pH conditions by the addition of various metal ions. It can be seen that Co2+ and Ni2+ can greatly quench the fluorescence of the PNPs in alkaline solutions, which is due to the improvement of the binding ability of the PNPs to Co2+ and Ni2+ under these conditions. In contrast, Fe3+ would cause a significant fluorescence quenching at the pH 3.0, and this might be because most Fe3+ cannot form hydroxides of iron in the pH solution. Fortunately, high quenching efficiency by Cu2+ is found under all the pH values. The good specificity of PNPs to Cu2+ at the condition of pH 4.0 is very similar to that of sensors developed by hPEI-protected Ag nanoclusters or hPEI-functioned carbon dots [8,18]. Therefore, the pH 4.0 was eventually chosen in the detection of Cu2+ for obtaining an excellent selectivity.

When utilizing the PNPs for practical sensing applications, there is no doubt that the stability of the material in solution with high ionic strength is very important. Thus we investigated the fluorescence of PNPs in different concentrations of NaCl solutions in the absence and presence of copper ions, respectively. As presented in Fig. S5, in solutions with different NaCl concentrations, the PNPs fluorescence intensity can keep stable and the levels of fluorescence quenching are almost the same by adding 10μM Cu2+, even in 1M NaCl solution, suggesting that the Cu2+ sensor may be able to fit a complicated environment.

Response time is another key factor in evaluating the practical application of a sensor. The response rate of the fluorescence signal of the PNPs to reaction time upon addition of Cu2+ was monitored subsequently. Fig. S6 reveals that the fluorescence quenching can be finished within 1min and a further increase of time does not cause further quenching of the fluorescence. This result demonstrates that the quenching of the PNPs fluorescence by Cu2+ is rapid and stable, implying a promising application in a fast and convenient sensing of Cu2+ without strict time control.

Under optimum conditions, the sensitivity was examined by recording emission spectra of the PNPs after adding different Cu2+ concentrations. Fig. 4 A exhibits that increasing the Cu2+ concentration gradually quenches the PNPs fluorescence, but no obvious change in the maximum emission wavelength was observed. Fig. 4 B shows the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of PNPs and the concentration of Cu2+, where the fluorescence intensity is decreased with increasing concentration of Cu2+. The intensity ratio F 0/F displays a good linear relationship (R 2 =0.996) versus Cu2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 8μM (inset of Fig. 4B) and it was easily described by the Stern-Volmer equation: F 0/F =1+ K sv[Q], where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the PNPs at 465nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, K sv is the Stern-Volmer fluorescence quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of Cu2+. K sv was calculated to be 1.9×105 M−1 by linear regression of the plot. The detection limit of Cu2+ was found to be 62nM on the basis of 3S b/slope (here S b represents the standard deviation of the background signals of the PNPs), which is 300 times lower than the maximum level of copper in drinking water (∼20μM) set by United States Environmental Protection Agency. In addition, we compared the developed fluorescent sensor with other optical sensors reported recently, for example, carbon dots-based or conjugated polymers-based fluorescent sensors and gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensors, in both material preparation and Cu2+ detection (Table S1). It can be seen that our fluorescence sensing platform has apparent superiority in preparation and also the sensitivity is not inferior to those of most other sensors. The results demonstrate that the PNPs can serve as an outstanding sensor for the sensitive detection of copper ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability and reliability of the proposed probe for Cu2+ detection, we applied the developed method to the analysis of Cu2+ in environmental water samples including tap water and Jialing River water in Chongqing. The tap water was used without any pretreatment, and the river water was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 30min to remove the insoluble impurities and then filtered through a 0.22μm membrane. The samples spiked with different concentrations of Cu2+ were measured according to the developed method. The results are listed in Table 1 . The recoveries are in the range of 103.5%-111.0%. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) obtained by repeating the experiment 6 times under the same conditions ranges from 1.18% to 3.86%, indicating a good repeatability of the proposed method. The recovery and RSD are satisfactory, which indicates the reliability of our method for Cu2+ detection in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cysteine-induced fluorescence enhancement and IMPLICATION logic gate
ce:section.ce:para: As an essential amino acid in natural proteins, Cys is involved in protein synthesis, detoxification, and metabolism in organisms and plays crucial roles in human health [44–46]. So the monitoring of Cys is very important in food, biological and medical fields. We explored the fluorescence enhancement of the PNPs-Cu2+ system induced by Cys in detail. Fig. S7A shows the effect of the level of Cys on the fluorescence recovery and it can be seen that the fluorescence gradually increases with increasing concentration of Cys. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of PNPs-Cu2+ and the concentration of Cys is shown in Fig. S7B. Notably, the enhancement of fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of Cys in the range from 0 to 40μM (R 2 =0.994) (inset of Fig. S7B, the linear equation: F/F 0 =1+1.2×106 C, here F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PNPs-Cu2+ in the absence and presence of Cys, respectively, and C is the concentration of Cys). The above results indicate that the Cu2+-PNPs system may have the ability to act as a platform for detection of Cys. We then evaluated the specificity to Cys. The effects of other essential amino acids on fluorescence of the PNPs-Cu2+ system were investigated, and the fluorescence change of PNPs-Cu2+ toward 20 essential amino acids at the same concentration (100μM) is presented in Fig. S7C. It can be clearly seen that only the addition of Cys can result in an apparent change, suggesting that the system possesses selective response ability toward Cys. Therefore, the PNPs-Cu2+ system holds great potential for fluorescence sensing of Cys in the fields of medicine and biotechnology.

Inspired by the fluorescence “on-off-on” conversion of the PNPs mediated by Cu2+ and Cys, we designed an IMPLICATION logic gate (Fig. 5 B shows the working symbol). Cu2+ and Cys were defined as the two inputs of the logic gate, and the fluorescence intensity at a wavelength of 465nm was defined as the output. For input, we defined the presence of Cu2+ or Cys as “1” state and their absence as “0” sate. For output, the common fluorescence of the PNPs was considered as “1” (on state), and the quenched fluorescence was defined as “0” (off state). Only with the input of Cu2+ and without Cys, the fluorescence is quenched, and the output is “0”. In all other states, the outputs are “1”. The truth table is displayed in Fig. 5A. We also investigated the reversibility of this logic gate operation. The repeated switching behavior with the alternating addition of Cu2+ and Cys is exhibited in Fig. 5C. Although the fluorescence of the PNPs is gradually decreased after the cyclic treatment with Cu2+ and Cys due to the dilution effect, the fluorescence switching (on/off) is still pretty obvious. The results demonstrate that the IMPLICATION logic gate operation is reversible, which is important for molecular logic gates in practical applications.

In conclusion, we have used a new kind of PNPs as a simple fluorescent sensor for fast and effectively sensing Cu2+ in aqueous media. The developed methods for detecting Cu2+ are facile, economic, and environment-friendly. The PNPs were prepared by the crosslinking of hPEI with formaldehyde and they had several attractive properties such as metal-free autofluorescence, ease of preparation, and good water solubility. The detection of Cu2+ is based on the principle that Cu2+ can be adsorbed on the surface and remarkably quenches the fluorescence of PNPs through electron transfer, and it is pretty sensitive and is hardly interfered by other metal ions. We applied the sensor to the analysis of copper ions in environmental water samples and satisfactory results were obtained. Furthermore, the fluorescence of PNPs-Cu2+ system can turn on by the addition of Cys which has such a strong binding ability to Cu2+ that it removes Cu2+ from the surface of PNPs. Based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” conversion, an IMPLICATION logic gate was successfully constructed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675131, 21273174), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (Nos. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001, CSTC-2013jjB00002), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYS16045).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.12.038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent sensors for measuring metal ions in living systems
Chem. Rev.
Recent developments in nanomaterial optical sensors
TrAC Trend. Anal. Chem.
Single probe for imaging and biosensing of pH, Cu2+ ions, and pH/Cu2+ in live cells with ratiometric fluorescence signals
Anal. Chem.
Adenosine capped QDs based fluorescent sensor for detection of dopamine with high selectivity and sensitivity
Analyst
Fluorescent nanoparticles for intracellular sensing: a review
Anal. Chim. Acta

Biosens. Bioelectron.
An unexpected role for the active site base in cofactor orientation and flexibility in the copper amine oxidase from Hansenula polymorpha
Biochemistry
Sensitive and selective detection of copper ions with highly stable polyethyleneimine-protected silver nanoclusters
Anal. Chem.
Environmental copper: its dynamics and human exposure issues
J. Toxicol. Environ. Heal. B
Copper homeostasis and neurodegenerative disorders (Alzheimer's prion, and Parkinson's diseases and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis)
Chem. Rev.
One-pot fabrication of polymer nanoparticle-based chemosensors for Cu2+ detection in aqueous media
Polym. Chem.
Colorimetric detection of Cu2+ by surface coordination complexes of polyethyleneimine-capped Au nanoparticles
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

Nanoscale

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shi Gang Liu is a M.S. candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yu Zhu Fan is a M.S. candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Nian Bing Li is a Professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and Ph.D. degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.

Hong Qun Luo is a Professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a Visiting Scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensors.
